result.question: Исследуй приложения и доказательные практики для реабилитации речи после инсульта (афазия), чтобы предложить, чем обогатить наше приложение. КОНТЕКСТ НАШЕГО ПРИЛОЖЕНИЯ: Flutter-планшетный тренажёр на русском языке для пожилого пациента (70+) после инсульта; самоучитель (занимается сам, без логопеда рядом); адаптивная «лестница» сложности (L0 узнавание → L1-L3); озвучка через TTS (без аудиозаписей и без строгого распознавания речи); доставка контента по воздуху (OTA). Уже есть типы упражнений: узнавание картинка→слово, да/нет слово↔картинка, слоги, соединение пар касанием, автоматизированные ряды, сборка слова из букв и предложения из слов, выбор формы слова, достраивание фразы (выбор/ввод), называние по описанию, обобщения, синонимы/антонимы, словообразование, паронимы, предлоги, окончания/падежи, ударение, вставка буквы, чтение текстов с вопросами, пересказ, слухоречевая память (ряды), часы/время. Есть безошибочный (errorless) режим, мягкий выход из лёгкого режима, подсказки и «не знаю». ВОПРОСЫ ИССЛЕДОВАНИЯ: (1) Какие ведущие/уважаемые приложения для афазии существуют (Tactus Therapy, Constant Therapy, Lingraphica, Cuespeak, Speech Sounds on Cue, Aphasia software finder и др.) и какие практики/механики в них есть, которых у нас нет? (2) Какие доказательные методики речевой реабилитации (errorless learning, spaced retrieval / distributed practice, cueing hierarchies / scaffolding подсказок, Melodic Intonation Therapy, semantic feature analysis, script training, constraint-induced) и как их реализуют в цифровых тренажёрах? (3) Практики вовлечённости и приверженности (adherence) для пожилых самостоятельных пользователей: дозирование, напоминания, обратная связь, геймификация без инфантилизации. (4) Доступность интерфейса для пожилых после инсульта (WCAG, размер целей, контраст, когнитивная нагрузка, гемианопсия/неглект). (5) Измерение прогресса и отчёты, полезные пациенту и его близким/логопеду. По каждому пункту — конкретные, применимые к нашему стеку (Flutter, TTS, OTA, без записи голоса) рекомендации с приоритетом. Отчёт на русском языке.

confirmed.claim: Retrieval practice (effortful retrieval of names) shows superior long-term retention at follow-up compared to errorless learning, except when targeting phonological-level impairments.
confirmed.quote: RP demonstrated superior long-term retention at follow-up intervals, except when targeting specific linguistic impairments at the phonological level.

confirmed.claim: Comparisons between errorless and errorful learning yield mixed results with no clear superior method; outcomes were comparable in several studies, with 2 studies favoring errorful and 2 favoring errorless.
confirmed.quote: Results were mixed. Several studies showed comparable outcomes, while others found advantages for errorful learning (2 studies) or errorless learning (2 studies).

confirmed.claim: Success and effort are the central treatment ingredients; spacing repeated trials increases retrieval difficulty while maintaining success, which enhances learning.
confirmed.quote: Spacing repeated trials increased retrieval difficulty while maintaining success, enhancing learning.

confirmed.claim: Self-managed, independent computerised SLT (StepByStep) practised at home for 20-30 min/day over 6 months improved word-finding by 16.2% more than usual care (CI 12.7-19.6%, p<0.0001), and most of the effect was maintained at 9 and 12 months — direct evidence that a self-managed digital aphasia trainer produces durable naming gains.
confirmed.quote: improved word-finding by 16.2% more than usual care (95% confidence interval 12.7% to 19.6%; p < 0.0001)." ... "Most of this effect was maintained at 9 and 12 months. Computerised speech and language therapy improvement in word-finding was broadly consistent regardless of time post stroke.

confirmed.claim: Naming gains did NOT generalise to functional communication or conversation; functional communication improvements were negligible and similar across groups, and only ~3 of 10 (vs ~1 of 10 controls) used five more treated words in conversation — implying a digital trainer must explicitly add functional-use/generalisation practice rather than rely on drill alone.
confirmed.quote: improvements in functional communication were negligible and very similar across groups." ... "85% of participants were assisted to practise using their new words in functional contexts for only 45 minutes (median) in total over 6 months

refuted.claim: Therapist/clinician support causally increases self-managed computer aphasia practice: each additional minute of therapist support added ~6 minutes (0.098 h) of total patient practice time, and increased support converged across qualitative and quantitative data as a key adherence driver.
Feedback on practice time presented as a color-coded calendar (yellow <20 min, green >20 min) motivated patients to practice longer.

The evidence-based dosage recommendation for self-managed aphasia computer therapy is 20-30 minutes per day over a 6-month period; actual practice averaged 28 hours over 6 months with very high variability (0-104.5 h).
Semantic Feature Analysis (SFA) improves naming of trained items in the large majority of treated aphasia patients (81.82%, 45 of 55 participants across 21 studies).
SFA produces positive outcomes robustly even when treatment procedures, dosage, duration, and protocol details vary, implying digital implementations have flexibility in dosing and protocol design.

Three quantitative factors independently predicted higher adherence to self-managed computer aphasia therapy: more time since stroke, longer access to the therapy software, and more therapist support time (each additional minute of therapist time predicted ~6 more minutes of practice).

More speech-language therapist support was the single factor confirmed by both quantitative and qualitative data as driving higher adherence, indicating that purely unsupported self-management tends to underperform.

Motivation factors that increased adherence included high self-efficacy, distal and proximal goal-setting, routine practice habits, and accurate performance feedback; capability/opportunity factors included personalized vocabulary and home-based therapy.

Software and hardware problems, especially voice recognition failures, were a key barrier lowering adherence, alongside cognitive impairment (memory, concentration, fatigue) and low computer confidence.

Clinician contact and monitoring directly improves adherence to self-administered tablet aphasia therapy; SLP time spent monitoring/adjusting software correlated with greater adherence, and lack of volunteer support reduced practice frequency.
Home/self-administered therapy achieves higher practice intensity than clinic-only therapy, with home users completing sessions at least every 2 days versus once every 5 days for clinic patients.

An asynchronous, gamified home neurorehabilitation program with only weekly therapist tele-sessions sustained high training adherence and delivered an average of 39.7 hours of active training time, with 82.2% of it done independently without a therapist present.

Decoupling training time from therapist presence did not compromise adherence, outcomes, or patient satisfaction over a 12-18 week program — supporting the viability of self-directed home practice with light remote supervision.

Older adults are motivated to engage with serious games primarily by 'meaningful learning' — knowledge that is practical and transferable to daily life — rather than by abstract scoring or play for its own sake.

Customization of difficulty across multiple dimensions (psychomotor, perceptual, visual, cognitive) and graduated challenge levels are critical design elements for sustaining engagement and adherence in older self-directed users.

Continuous, real-time feedback with guided instructions reduces confusion and frustration for older users, while complex controls increase cognitive load; touch screens are preferred over complex controllers.

Avoiding infantilization requires coherent, respectful narratives free of cliché and pessimism, with motivational recognition that lets older users feel valued (e.g., teaching/sharing wisdom) rather than condescended to.

Interface design for older stroke survivors should use a 'care mode' with large clear text, vivid colors, good recognition, and large buttons — directly applicable accessibility recommendations for a 70+ tablet trainer.

Content should use everyday language instead of academic/medical terminology, limit text to a maximum of three screens, keep fonts uniform in size and color, and use animations/illustrations as visual aids to reduce cognitive load for elderly users.

The paper's recommendations target YOUNG stroke survivors under 55 years (and their carers), not elderly patients — a population mismatch with our 70+ target user, so recommendations should be applied with caution and re-validated for older users.
